And so basically what I look at is the concept is how to and then what do what do my people desire the most and then without the thing that causes them the most pain. So for example, when we sold our high ticket secrets course, we were helping people. A lot of people wanted to sell high ticket things. They didn't want to do it on the phone, right? So they wanted high ticket things. So my headline is like how to sell really, really expensive high ticket things. But then like the fear was like, I don't want to be on the phone. Like I'm scared because I'm the same way. Like I'm so scared of the phones. Like there's a reason why now you guys talk to me on the phone before you were sold because if you did, you wouldn't be here. Cause I would have talked to you out of buying. Cause I'm the worst on the phone ever. Like it scares me more than anything. So the vehicle they're all in is like, I got to call people on the phone and sell them coaching. I was like, no, that vehicle sucks. Like how to sell super expensive things without ever picking up the phone personally. And that was like my new vehicle and that offer crushed it for us and still does awesome. Okay. So it was like, that was how to where they desire most without what causes them the most pain. So funnel scripts. I use the exact same headline as well. How to get all of your sales letters, video sales, letters, PowerPoints and everything created without. What's my headline now? Does anyone have it open without, without hiring an extensive copywriter or learning how to write copy yourself. Something like that. Okay, so now that we got kind of that bridge from yesterday till today, we're going to start shifting more and more into story. So I got one of the things I want to share first before we kind of go into story structure. And this is something that at the first time we all met and talked about this kind of stuff last summer, we ended up a thousand make a 10 minute session and taken three hours. But I think we kind of figured it out. So this comes back to a concept that I call in the book and I call a lot of places called the one thing or the big domino. Okay. And so a couple of stories that kind of set this up. So it makes sense. When I was talking to Perry Belcher, I was having a conversation one late night. He had a little bit to drink, which is makes it way easier to get like all the good stuff and people right. And I'm, you know, the Mormon dude who doesn't drink. So I'm like, actually like, oh yeah, everyone's drunk and they share all the best stuff then. Right. The big benefit of like hanging out after hours when you don't drink. So I'm listening to all this stuff and I'm getting all these things right. And I'm asking Perry and I'm like, so like, because he's one of the best copywriters I've ever met. And I was like, what are the things you're learning? What's what have you figured out? I said, the biggest thing that we've learned, and this is Perry, his company, but also like digital marketers and hold everything. It's just, he said, the biggest thing we found, all the offers we've ever created and anytime our message tried to convince them of more than one thing, they bombed. He said, if we try to give him some, convince him two things or three things, like they always didn't work. He said, the ones that all the blockbuster wins, the only thing we were trying to do is get them to believe one thing through the sales message. And if we had the one consistent, it was that it's one thing. And I started looking at, I was looking at a lot of my things I was trying to sell at the time. Like I'm trying to convince him of this on this and then I'm trying to convince him of all these different things. And as soon as you do that, like it waters it down and just it stops sales instantly. And I like, when I heard that, I thought, God, that's really, really cool. And then a couple months later, there was another event and Tim Ferriss was on stage talking and he's doing this whole thing. And you're gonna hear him speak in person. He's a great writer. Like he's so boring. Like as a speaker, I was just like, ah, and I was kind of like, anyway, it was kind of painful to sit through to be completely honest. And the end of it, like Q&A and I was like listening and some lady came up and she's like, I have a question for you, Tim. Like, what does your normal day look like? And I was like, that's actually a really cool question. I was really excited to hear the answer to it. Right. And so he's like, okay, well, he's like, I've actually had a bunch of people who have asked me if they could like come and film a day in the life of Tim Ferriss and see what I do. He's like, like he told him, no, because if you saw what I actually did every day, it's like insanely boring. He's like, I wake up in the morning, I drink some coffee, then like I'll read a book. And I think for a while and then I meditate and then I go get my blood drawn and I go back and I think some more and I meditate and then he's like, I'll do that for three or four or five weeks at a time. Before I do anything. And he's like, what most people do, they wake up every morning, they got to do this and try to do a thousand different things and they're going through everything and try and knock down all these different to do's and get them all done. He's like, my philosophy is I want to sit there and I want to think for a long time and figure out what's the one big domino that if I knock that domino down. You knock down all the other dominoes and makes them irrelevant. And that's there's only lessons from like a personal development like destruction, but I was thinking about from a cell standpoint how similar it is as well. Like I can go try to convince them a thousand different things, but what if there was one thing if I can convince you of this one thing. That it is the big domino that it automatically knocks everything else down and makes any other concerns or objections or anything else you have irrelevant or it makes them just disappear. Like what is that one thing. So every sales message that we're creating. I'm like, what is what's the one thing? If I can figure out that one thing, then what's the one big domino that we figured that out. Everything else disappears. And so that's kind of and when you come we go deep in the perfect webinar, you'll see that it's all based on this. Like the perfect webinar is not convincing you of like one thing and three of our secrets and three secrets are all like everything is to knock down the one thing. The one big domino. And so like that's what we got to figure out in every single thing. So I started going through this. I'm like, well, how do we because in theory that's it's easy, right? Okay, cool. I need one thing, but to actually figure that out. It's a little more difficult. And so last summer when we were talking about this, you guys remember we're on the whiteboard and we were going through like 20 different variations trying to figure it out. And finally we got things to fit into this sentence. And this is the big domino sentence. So it says, if I can make them believe that my new opportunity is the key to what they desire most and it's only attainable through my specific vehicle, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. Okay, so that's the that's the structure of the framework. I make a new offer, a new product, whatever it is. I'm trying to fit it into that. If I can fit into that, then I'm like, this is gonna be homerun. I have no doubt like this is going to be this is going to be perfect. Okay, so a couple examples to kind of show how this works. So in my company, like if I can make them believe that funnels is key to their online business success and is only attainable through click funnels, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant. They have to give me money. If I can honestly convince you that the only way you can be successful is with the funnel and click funnels is the only way you can get a funnel. You can be expensive like, well, do you want to be successful? You have to have it. Oh, well, it's technical. Well, it's the only way to do it. You have to like there. You have to say yes. There's no other alternative because if I can get you to believe that that you're either lying to yourself or you have to give me money. Like that's the only outcomes. There's no other way around it. Right. So that's what we have to create. Okay. Now, when we started doing this last summer, everyone's like, cool, I got it and they sent me back a thing. So I'm going to show you like what I got back from about half of you guys. Something like something like this. Like all other stuff doesn't actually work. Right. And that's what we have to create. So it takes a little while to think through it. First off, and this goes back to stuff I talked about yesterday. First off means you're not in the prolific zone. Second thing, you are not offering a new opportunity and you're not in a blue ocean. So the first thing, and I know we've talked about this a lot in the inner circle, but I want to share with everyone again, just so, so important. So kind of the backstory on how this started coming together in my head. I've talked about prove it a couple of times. So prove it is a network marketing company that sells little packets of white powder. Like look like this and you drink it and then you put your body in ketosis within like five minutes, right? And so my buddy started this company and I've been in a company with him prior called Ripplin. They remember Ripplin. So he created this company. He signed. I closed you guys before I even knew you. So he started this company. It was a really cool idea. He had me come in. I did all the videos and the like a story, all kinds of stuff. And we launched it went from zero to 1.5 million people within a six week period time. And it was like giving me my big payday and then they didn't ever make the product, which sucked and the whole thing imploded. And I lost like six months of my life, but I learned some good lessons and made some good friends. And I realized I would never do network marketing again, no matter how much money somebody paid me. So that was how that whole thing ended. And then like two years later, Brian is going and calls me and say, Hey, man, I got my next thing. Like, you want to be part of it? I was like, No dude, I will not ever be part of that again. Like I went through a lot of pain and that was not fun. And he's like, Oh, you got him. And we got this new thing is trying to pitch me on this, on this thing. Right. And I'm like, No dude, like I love you, but I'm, I got my own stuff happening. So anyway, then he got bugging and kept bugging me. And then one day he's like, what's your address again? And I told him and then like two days later I get in the mail like this postal envelope and I open it up and there's two white baggies full of white powder with magic marker written that says keto OS on it. I'm pulling out these two baggies with nothing, no instructions, no anything, just random white baggies. And I'm like, Okay, I don't know if this is, but I'm really like nervous. And then I'm like, do what's this? He's like, Oh, just try it. They try it. I'm like, I don't know if I trust you enough to just like get a bag of white powder and drink it because you told me to try it. Like that's kind of freaky. And anyway, so I didn't, I put it in the envelope next to like my fridge and it was there for like two or three weeks. And then one day I was heading to church and woke up in the morning late and we got five kids, we're getting them all ready. And I was leaving out the door and I didn't have time to eat. And I was hungry and Mormon church is long. It's like three hours and there's meetings and like it's a big deal. Right. So I'm like, crap, I don't know how to eat. And I saw like the magic powder like glowing like, Oh, so I went and I'm like, okay, I'll dry it. So I put it in, shake it up, drink it and it tasted like candy. I was like, this is awesome. And I went through the church the whole day and everything else came on that night. And I was like, I'm not hungry. I have energy like whatever it was, like I was, I was like, this is really good. So I called Brian back up. I'm like, Hey man, that was awesome. I want more of that of the magic white powder. And just usually how I think most of these things start, right? And he's like, we don't have any. Like that was some samples described me six, eight weeks before I get any more. And I was like, dude, you can't like, like if a drug dealer gave you, like, anyway, I'm just assuming that's not anyway. So I'm like freaking out. I'm like, I want some more, I want some more. So finally a couple months later, he gives me some more and he's like, you should really be part of this. I'm like, no, I'm not going to be part of it. So I'm laying in bed at two in the morning. I just highlighted the whole thing and I deleted it. And I wrote out a script real quick, sent it back to him. He's like, this is amazing. Or maybe it was someone talked about you make money and that was the something happened or like got you excited. And you're like, oh, well, the reason why this works is because in fact, I'm looking at the sea of people and like they all had shirts on that said like ketones is greater than glucose. And like all of them had these shirts on and like I'm looking at these people and I was like, you guys are such an urge. Your socks like and they're all like these people are pouncing like they see their potential prospect walk by right. And they're looking and they're like, okay, this is my chance. It's going to be so good. And then they pounce on the first thing they do is they start spewing out all this techno babble like, you need this, you need this. And like, this is ketosis. This is why you're in the doughnuts. That's sugar. And then you're like, oh, this is crap. And like yelling at me like, and the person likes watching this and freaks out right. Like, leave me alone. Right. And that's how most selling happens is this right here because we, we had this experience. We bought all in, we geeked out, we learned all the stuff. And then we come to our potential customers. We start spewing techno babble down on them. And that's, that's why most people suck at selling. That's it. Okay. And I told these people in the group. I was like, did any of you guys join this program because somebody convinced you that glucose was less than ketones? Like no. And then actually asking a bunch of questions like, who here excited for the company because of the beta hydroxybutyrate salts in the thing? And like nobody, like, then why are you telling everybody about this crap? Nobody cares. I was like, what was the thing that gave you the original epiphany that got you excited? Cause that is the key. Like that's what you got to figure out. When Brian sent me that script that night, I read it and he was explaining ketosis, all technical and all these things. And I was like, I don't know what that means. But what I did remember is about six months earlier, I was at, I was at an event with, it was a marketing event. And after the event, I went out to dinner with one of my friends named Aaron Lilly. And he's like cut and ripped and everything. We're sitting there eating and he ordered, I ordered like really good food. And then he ordered chicken with a side of butter and he's eating chicken, dipping it in butter. And I was like, dude, you are the weird, like it was so weird to me. I'm like, why are you doing that? He's like, oh, well, my body's in ketosis. I'm like, what does that mean? He's like, well, how do I explain it to Russell? He's like, well, it's kind of like, imagine you had a campfire, right? And a campfire is burning. If you get like kindling, you throw it on what happens. I'm like, it burns really fast and it goes away. He's like, yes, that's like carbs. That's why we eat cheerios for breakfast. It like burns really fast and they go away really fast. I'm like, okay. And he's like, he's like, proteins are kind of like the kindling or kind of like logs. They can throw those on, they burn a lot longer, but eventually they still burn out. He's like, fat is like coals. It's like the coals that if you can catch the fat on fire, like they burn all night long. He's like, my body only burns coals. That's why I'm ripped and I'm shredded. He's like, you're burning off carbs. That's why you're hungry at the time. You're energy goes up and down all these things. I was like, huh. And so that's what explained Catoces to me. So after I deleted Brian's script, I was like, why, like, why does this matter to me? And I was like, I remember the story that Aaron told me about that game media epiphany, why the whole Catoces conversation was interesting. And so I wrote the script. And if you guys have ever seen it, I wrote, it's the big call inside the company, the campfire video. And I have a thing like, hey, if you're a campfire, it's kind of like, and I have the whole thing explaining my epiphany. And that's what sells it is this. And so for all of you guys, whatever business you're in, there's something that happened. There's some epiphany or some story that happened that got you guys excited about what you do. Right. For me, there was a reason why I got interested in funnels. There's a reason why I got interested in wrestling. Like something happened that caused you to have that epiphany. Like, dang, that is cool. I'm going to go and this is like something I want to become part of, right? My first question, do you guys remember that? You don't get sometimes we forget because it's been so long, but do you remember the experience you had? Because that's like the key to everything we're talking about the rest of today is that that experience that gave you the initial epiphany. And we got to come back over here and we got to come to this is where our customers are potential customers are at. If I bring any of this crap over here, they're not going to buy. Okay. If I can come back here and I can give them an epiphany and tell them a story and they have the same epiphany, it's done. They'll go geek out on their own. They'll figure it all out. They'll be all in. I just have to give them this epiphany that I had. That's the key. Okay. So first thing is I'm kind of going through this. I want you guys thinking back because there's different stories. We're going to be after lunch, after lunch, we're doing a whole story workshop. We're going to be building out a ton of stories. But the first one, what are you thinking about? Like what was your origin story? Okay. Every good superhero has got an origin story. I'm going to skip forward and then backwards. I want to do this last. So the next thing I want to talk about is what's important when you're telling stories is we have to get people to feel what we felt. Okay. I hope Tony Robbins talks about this at Funnel Hacking Live. They asked me like, I want them to speak on. So I gave them a list of all the cool stuff. But one of the biggest things I learned from Tony was like state control. Like how to get yourself into a peak state or a sad, whatever state you want to do. Like how to get into state. Right. And so I started looking at this and kind of like trying to understand how it all works. I realized that like if I just tell somebody my, my epiphany story, sometimes they'll get it since they won't. Right. And you guys have had experience where like you have this really good idea or a story happened and you tell someone you explained the whole thing to them and they're like, Oh, wow, that sounds cool. You're like, no, no, no, like, dude, you like, oh, you try to re-explain it. And they're like, no, no, I get it, man. That sounds really cool. You're like, you try three or four times and find like, God, if you would have been there, like it would make total sense. Right. And so that's what a lot of us do when we tell our stories. We tell this whole story and then, and then people are like, Oh, cool. Yeah. You had an epiphany. You think you should campfire or get it. No, it's cool. Like they, they heard the story, but they didn't have the epiphany because they were in the wrong state. They weren't in the state that you were in. So we have to create an environment where we're telling stories where we get people into the same state that we were in as close as possible. Cause if I can get them in the same state that I was in when I had that epiphany, they're much more likely to have the same epiphany. That's why some people are really good storytellers and anything. They say you're like, die man. Like I'm, I'm signing up and some people it's like, yeah, that sounds cool. I guess I had to be there. Right. So how do we do that? So the big thing I want you guys to think through is this. It's called state control. How do we control their state? Okay. And it's all through how we actually tell the story. So instead of just saying that, like I used to say, if I was to tell a story about the, one of my stories was, or is, when I first started hiring employees and I had like 13 of my friends I hired and we were totally losing money and it was Christmas. It was like two weeks for Christmas and I was out hanging up Christmas lights and I really thinned code on. I remember like, I remember putting it on, but like the wind kept blowing and it's like cutting through my, I gotta even fill the code. It was like cutting through me and I had this little staple gun because I didn't know how to hang up Christmas lights. It was my first year and I got this little wobbly ladder and I remember I didn't have gloves on because I wanted to use the staple gun and I was like holding it. It was freezing cold. My hands were like so cold and I touched the thing, but I had to go up so I was kind of like hiding it and I was climbing up this thing and I got the staple gun out and I'm listening. I was stapling these Christmas lights and as I'm doing it, I'm listening on my headphones to a marketing conference because at that time my entire company was about to go bankrupt. It was two weeks before Christmas and I knew that the next morning I was gonna have to call, at the time, my friends and my family members and basically I had to let them go two weeks before Christmas because not only did I have money to cover their paychecks for the last two weeks, I had no money for the next two weeks. As I thought about firing my friends and two of my brothers and potentially letting go other people that I had so much to say for, I was sitting there hanging this up and this feeling in my gut started coming and I felt like a heart attack, but it wasn't here. It was down lower and it was just like this stabbing pain and I almost couldn't lift my hands up to staple because I kept feeling that. I remember feeling this pain, but at the same time I was freezing cold, but my hands were sweating and I couldn't understand it because it was like, why is it so cold? Why did I have sweat coming from my hands? So much like I barely hold on this thing. I think like, how in the world am I gonna call these guys tomorrow and let them know that there's no Christmas bonus. It's not even Christmas. Like, sorry, you're not gonna have money to pay for your wife and your kids. Like, what a letdown. I remember feeling like the weight of the world on my shoulders were like, I just didn't want to even go back inside. I spent hours out there hanging lights, listening to this marketing thing, hoping for some ray of hope, something I could do that would give us the freedom so we could continue business. There's a whole story after that, but I'm gonna tell it that way. Did you feel a little bit more? How many of you guys thought like your palms are sweating right now? And so most of us, like you got to come back and explain, not just like here's the thing that happened, but it's explaining how you felt. So I don't tell them, I'm leading them to this thing. I need them to have the epiphany, not me telling them the epiphany. If I just give it to them, they're going to miss it. Okay? And that company, Ripplin, I'm going to find it real quick. One of the guys I was working with on the concept, when we rolled that out, he'd written a script. Initially, he was like, here's the script. I hope I can find it. And it was just like a, it was a horrible script. And I remember, I remember, I remember the book, they were giving people the epiphany. And it was such like a network marketing pitch. And I remember, I emailed that dude that night, and I put it in the book. I thought it was important. I emailed him. I said, hey man, what I found is that when you're just selling something, it's not as strong. It doesn't create the emotion you need to really cause action. If you want people to adopt a new concept and really get their buy-in, you have to lead them to the answer. But you can't give it to them. They have to come up with the idea of themselves. You plant the idea in their minds with a story, and if they come up with the answer, then they sold themselves. The buy-in decision becomes theirs, not yours. When that happens, you don't sell them anything. That's the goal I was trying to do. We're creating story so that they go on this journey with me, and all of a sudden they're like, dang, I need that thing. And you're like, yes, you do. And then it's off, and at that point, four is all them. So that's this whole story stuff, like, so exciting. But that's the power behind this, you guys. Okay, now a couple really quick things we put in the book that I think are important I want to share. One thing is, as you asked us now, before, there's technical things a lot of times we have to share. And so for me, what I do, every time I'm explaining anything, and you probably noticed it the last day and a half, I'll go into something and I'll be telling the story. And also, I hit something I have to share because it's part of the thing, right? So like, when I did the pitch for Prove It, I say, like, da-da-da-da, key tones. And as soon as I hit that word key tones, I know that everyone's like, boom, techno babble, and everyone's like, Instinct is just like, zone out. So every time I'm telling a story, I hit a technical thing, I stop right there, and I do what I call kind of like bridge. I say, it's kind of like a million motivational speakers running through your body giving you insane amounts of energy. And they're like, oh yeah, that's pretty, like, that's how I do it. So every time I hit any kind of concept that I think people are going to be stuck on, I stop, and I'm like, it's kind of like, boom, and that's like my bridge, every time I hit something. So I'm telling the story, there's going to be technical things in this journey. Every time I hit that, I stop and I do a kind of like bridge. And it's just really quick thing like, new concept, what do they already understand? The new concept, what is it something that it's kind of like that they can already understand? And as soon as you bridge that, they're like, oh, this makes perfect sense. Thank you so much. And that's the key. Like they're just, they're super fast. And it's, every time you have a technical babble, it has to slip out for them to understand a piece. The second slips out, I switch, boom, it's kind of like this, and then they understand. So a few years ago, Dagen Smith and I did an event called the Invisible Funnel together. It was in DC, and I think he has all the access to it in the inner circle members area. It's an older one, but it was amazing. We had this guy come out and speak. His name is Michael Hague. You were there? Yes. I think your only person was there. Todd was there. Yes, two people were there. It was good. So Michael Hague, we pay this dude from Hollywood to come and speak to us. His name is Michael Hague, and he's got a course. I told you guys about this. Some of you guys about this. It's called the hero's two journeys. And him and this guy, Chris Vogler, go through this concept called the hero's two journeys. And he came and spoke at our event and walked us through this concept. And then for the last like 10 years, or five years, he's been like percolating through my brain and trying to figure out how does what he shared like relate to what we do. And it took me a long time to like bridge that gap, but it's like the coolest thing in the world. So the hero's two journeys based on like 98% of all Hollywood films fit into this structure. And what Michael Hague was telling us is basically like, you look at any movie, he's like from a screen, from a screenwriter standpoint, you know that every single movie in like page X of the script, the hero always does this. On page this in the script, they always do this. And it's like the same. And you can plug any movie, almost any movie, into this process, which is fascinating. He's like this storytelling at its roots. Like this is every movie that's ever become big, all follows this. And the majority of all movies, all stories do. And so he explained it all. I was like, oh, that's so cool. But you know, we're not writing Hollywood movies. Like how do we actually make that something tangible that we can do? And so that's the next section. I'll be talking about that. But first of all, you guys understand this piece of it because when you understand this piece of it, then it makes your actual storytelling so much cooler and so much easier. Okay. All right. So I'm going to go through this piece by piece by piece. All right. So the first thing that he talked about is, and here's two journeys, that basically every good story has three core things. There's a character. The character has desires. And then on his way to get those desires, there's conflict. That's it. Okay. That's also like in his most basic primal state, that is what story is. Three things. Character, desire, conflict. Okay. And so, for example, the example he shared when he spoke at our event was Little Red Riding Hood. Little Red Riding Hood, actually next thing, Little Red Riding Hood has a desire to take a basket of cookies for grandma. But on the journey there, there's conflict. There's a big bad wolf who wants to eat her. That's the story. So that's the plot statement, right? Let's go back. So all the things happened. And one thing he talked about, he said that most people think that the emotion in a story is caused by the desire because for you, for the hero, like your desire is like, oh, I want that thing. I want to win this thing, right? See this desire you're going for? And that's what causes emotion for you. But nobody cares about the thing the hero's trying to get. Like what causes the emotion is the conflict in the middle. That's what makes the story actually interesting is the desire, is the conflict. So without conflict, it's not a good story. So there's always character. There's desire and there's conflict. So again, here's the Red Riding Hood plot statement. So I want you guys to think back about your origin story, how you had your first initial epiphany about what it is that you do right now. And why you think of it in these three things. There's a character, which is probably you. There's the desire you had, which is to make money, lose weight, invest in whatever your thing is. What's the desire? And then on your journey to get that thing, what was the conflict that you ran into? So all you guys are now working on is we're going to write a plot statement. It's basically this. So if mine was Russell Brunson, I had a desire to be able to make enough money. My wife could quit her job. And the conflict I met along the way is it turns out it's really hard to build websites. And I had all these issues that came along, right? Super simple. So everyone, let's take a minute and write a plot statement for your origin story, the thing that got you to where right now. Character, desire, and conflict. I had a burning desire to build a business and network marketing. I just felt compelled. It was something I was just aching to do inside, but I didn't know how to be a mom and to be a successful business owner. And what I wanted to bless my family's life started taking me away from them. Cool. That's a good conflict. Awesome. Thank you. Two more shares. Whoa, that was close. The character is my mom, Dr. Jennifer Daniels. I think her desire was to help people or cure people. And her conflict was just the whole medical-industrial complex. And she has to know that her real story is insane. All right. Nick. Guy's transitioning out of the military. He wants to be successful like he was in the service. Little did he know there were nine transition traps waiting to gobble him up. Nice. Cool. All right. So that's the first thing I'm going to figure out in any story is the plot line, okay, is that. So like, the nice thing about stories, you can tell a story in like 10 seconds or like 10 hours, like just by filling in the details of all this kind of stuff. Okay. So as you come back through, so with the character, there are a couple of things that Michael Hague taught me that's like, for every character in a movie, there are basically, there's five things that you can do to build rapport with the audience. And the character needs at least two of these five. And typically the first 10% of every single movie, the backstory, the origin story is all about like getting you to actually care about the character. Because if you don't care about the character, then this whole journey just becomes boring and people don't want to, they don't care. So the first 10% of the movie is all about building rapport with the hero. Okay. So they do it through, again, five things and they try to get at least two of these five things. So either the person needs to be a victim, which makes you care about them. They need to have like something in jeopardy, their life in jeopardy, their job in jeopardy, something that's going to be falling apart. They got to be likable, they got to be funny, or they got to be powerful. Because you think that like there's two, like two of those things that are going to be introduced to your audience for them to have rapport with you enough to actually care about your story. If there's not, they're like, if you guys watch, I remember watching, it's one of the like end of the world movies. I remember watching it and like the end of the character died and I was like, I don't even care. Like I'm glad he died. He was kind of annoying. I remember the whole movie was ruined for me because there was no like, I didn't care. So the big thing is you have to care about the character or else the rest of the story doesn't even matter. Okay. So it's all about building rapport. And those are, again, he said in movies that when they try to, they try to get two of the five. And the second thing is then there's the desire where they're trying to get. He says that typically the desire is one of four things. They're trying to win something when a race, when a, when a girl, when a whatever, just going to win something. We're trying to retrieve something, trying to go somewhere and get something and bring it back. We're trying to escape from some bad guys. We're trying to stop something. Think about most movies. You think you got any movie you can plug through this, Disney movies, real movies, superhero movies. Like they're always, the desire is one of those four things typically. So it's like, what's the desire of the character? I want to win this thing. I want to stop whatever that thing might be. Okay. So for your story, it's the same thing like when you went on this journey, for your origin story, like which one of those was it for you? You're trying to retrieve something, trying to escape from some, some life you hated. Trying to stop something bad from happening. Okay. So now you come back here to this slide. So this is kind of showing, I'm going to keep building on this slide each time. So now we've got the three core things in the plot line. Here's the attractive character. And here's how we build rapport. Like I said, the first 10% of every single movie. So he was saying like on page, whatever it was, 22 in the screenplay is always the first 10%. And that's when the hero leaves, like physically leaves where they're at. Frodo leaves the Shire, right? Lightning McQueen leaves the racetrack. Bell leaves her house. Like it's always like the character is leaving. So the initial, they're there in this environment where you're understanding the character, you're building rapport with them. And then the character actually physically leaves. There's really a lot of my videos. If you're noticed, like, I'm not just standing at my desk. I'm physically leaving like, Hey, I'm getting in my car. Come on guys, let's go. And I jump and I'm leaving. So I'm trying to take somebody with me on a journey. Like I'm physically leaving. Frank Kern is great at this too. He always watches the videos. He's never there in front of desk. He's like, Hey guys, how's it going? Come on over here. And he's leaving and bringing people on a journey with them. I was talking to Dan during the break. There's a video I want to make here where like someone opens the door and they're coming to click phones. And I welcome to click phones and I take them and we walk to a different place. And as soon as they're doing that, they're following you on this journey. So I don't know if I'm doing it like as I'm speaking, but like a lot of times physically in the video or however you're using it, I'm leaving on this journey. Right. Now, what's happening is that again, this is called the heroes two journeys. The first journey is what we call the journey of achievement. This is what the heroes setting out to they want to do this thing. Right. And that's what they're always thinking about. Like I got to go win this race. I find like this thing's happening. So this is like what's very known to the audience. The audience knows like Frodo's leaving season and take the ring. He's going to leave the shy. He's coming over here. He's got to drop into the volcano and destroy the ring. Like that's the, that's the journey of achievement. I got to achieve this end goal to get my desires. Right. And then the second journey down here is called the journey of transformation. This is the journey that we're not aware of by watching the outside. This is what's happening where the character actually is transforming as a person. Okay. So the two journeys journey of achievement and the journey of transformation. Okay. Okay. Now if you come back here. This is the journey of transformation. The second journey was happening is during the backstory of the character, you're learning about their identity. Like this is who I am. This is why I believe that I am like, I believe these things are important to me. All stuff's happening. Right. Think about any movie like these are the things are important to me. And then as they leave and they go on this journey, all this conflict starts happening and all their beliefs, like the things that they hold true themselves, like the identity they have, they start realizing that it's not good or it's wrong or something. And they go through this journey. All these things start falling apart to eventually at the end of it, the journey of transformation happens and they become a new person. They have a new belief system because of this journey. Right. So if you look at this from the heroes to journeys, this journey right here, journey of achievement down here is the journey of transformation where you're leaving your own identity. So I'm going to kind of tell you guys a story from a movie and I'm going to plug it into this framework. So you can kind of see the whole thing. Okay. I'm going to share it with the movie from car or movie cars. How many of you have seen cars like 50 times? So good. So lightning McQueen is the character. He has a desire to win the piston cup. He runs in a conflict when he gets stuck in radiator strings. Right. Boom. There's a storyline. So the first 10% of the movie, we are at a certain location where the character feels safe, feels very, very good. Things are happening and we're introduced to him. Right. So start going through and things are happening. Right. So we see he's powerful. Right. He's kind of funny. He's doing things like that. Right. And we're building this rapport with him. You're seeing him racing to the first rookie ever to win the piston cup. It's all exciting. You have this whole backstory. And then he loses it or they tie. Right. They three way tie. It ends. And so because that he has to go on this journey. If he wants to achieve his thing, he wants to win the piston cup. I got to go on this journey. I have to go retrieve the piston cup. I have to go win it. Like I have to leave. Right. So he gets in Mac gets in the car and we have this really cool glimpse of lightning McQueen that makes us actually care about him. Because the first is like this cool powerful guy. It's super awesome. They get in the car and then his agent gets on the phone and he starts talking to the agent and the agent basically lets in this little glimpse. We know that lightning McQueen is actually super insecure and nobody really likes him. And that's why he's so powerful. But like he has no friends. He's like, Hey, I got tickets for your friends. You don't want me to give him to you. He's like, Oh, well, you're going to be the right. I'm not going to actually make it, but I'll be watching your band. And like, we realize like, wow, he's actually vulnerable. Like we start seeing the character flaws in lightning McQueen right? Wow. Like this is thing happens. Right. So then 10% of the movie now we've got, we actually care about this character. We've got some relationship with right. And then he leaves. He's got to go on this journey of achievement. He's leaving and all of a sudden conflict happens. Right. And he gets stuck in your ear springs. He's locked up and he's going through all these things and he's trying to win. He's trying to get to the thing. He's got to get your wind piston cup over and over and over again. And all this conflict is happening thing after thing after thing and all this conflict is happening. And throughout the entire movie, you can see all this conflict happening over and over and every time he tries to leave, he gets caught. This thing happens. This thing happens. And at the end of it, finally he, he gets to go to the, to the race. He shows with the piston cup. He goes out there and he's racing and he's kind of miserable. And he's not having them. Like he's, he's doing well, but he's not really that happy. And then doc and all those guys come and they become the sideline. Right. So he goes out there and he's doing this whole race and he's getting a point where he's actually has a shot. A shot to actually win this thing that he's been trying to, trying to get his whole entire life. And there's a point like he crashes and there's no way he can actually win. And he comes back and he goes back through it again and finds the point. He's about to win. He's racing and he comes down to the finish line and he's just screaming through. He's beating everybody. Comes to the finish line, looks up to the monitor and he notices something just happened. Chick Hicks, who's like the jerk of the whole thing. He hits the king who he's racing. That's right. Hits the king. The king's car flips out of control, gets completely destroyed, smashed up into little pieces. He looks up to the monitor and he sees the king, smashed on the side and realizes he's not going to finish this race. And all of a sudden his achievement becomes in jeopardy. The coolest thing about the hero's two journeys, sometimes the hero achieves their thing. Sometimes they don't. What's interesting, we don't really care if they achieve this. What's more interesting is the transformation that happens to the character down here. So what happens? The line between sees that. He slams on his brakes. It comes all the way to finish line, stops. And Chick Hicks flies past him, wins the race. Lightning Queen backs up, comes over to the king, gets behind him and starts pushing him to the finish line. And the king says, dude, what are you doing? He's like, you just lost yourself to Piston Cup. And then Lightning Queen says, oh, you know, a friend of mine once told me, you know, it's just an empty cup. It's not that big a deal. And he pushes the king across and we have a chance to see that not only he misses his achievement, but he transformed as a person. That's the real journey. That's the journey we actually cared about was the journey of transformation. Isn't that awesome? I have wheels. Yeah. I was going through this thing a little while ago and I mapped it out and I was trying to figure out movies and I thought about that one. I was just like, how cool is that? Most of us, we're always trying to achieve this thing, achieve this thing, achieve this thing. We think that's what people actually care about, but it's not. We didn't care if Lightning Queen hit it or not. We thought about that, like him taking this insecure person with no friends and through the story, he came something different, became someone more. That's what we care about. That's why we love that movie. That's why I watch like 30 times with my kids because of that. That's the cool thing. It's the second journey. So that's kind of the framework of most movies. So I want you guys, I want you guys to think back through one of the last movies you saw, which I want you to remember the best. So my next question that after like, there's like a five year thing for me because I learned all stuff from him. I'm like, that's awesome. But like, what's the structure for me to like tell this in a room where I've got maybe two minutes or I've got 10 minutes or I've got an hour. Like, how do I do that? And I've tried over the years, like figure out different, different processes. In fact, even in the inner circle, I think I've shared two or three different versions of what of the new script. And this is the one like a week and a half ago when I was writing at three in the morning, also I was like, oh my gosh, like it encompasses everything. It took like five or six of my stories and I ran through this. I was like, this is like the first time where I was just like freaking out. So I don't think any of you guys have actually seen this image yet. This is the most up to date version, the one that I am like super excited for of the actual Pifny Bridge script. So this is the magic. And this is going to be the template that all my stories run through now. And it's just, it makes this whole concept simple. I want to share the heroes to journey. So you guys see from a higher, like here's how the whole thing works. How do we do it for like our intents and purposes? So these are kind of the different pieces of it. I'm going to walk through it all because if you get it all, like it's just really powerful. So first thing, first key is we always start with some kind of backstory, right? And part of it is like we talked about heroes to journey. You have to, people have to care who you are. And the problem is with you as an expert, they're seeing you up on this mountain. They're like, dang, like, look how awesome this person is. And if you're up there talking down to them, then they don't, there's no empathy. They don't care about you as much. So like for you to have, to have real empathy and impact on someone, you have to get off of wherever you're at. And the results you've already achieved and come back down over here to where they're at. And be like, Hey man, guess what? I was in this exact same spot. I remember what it was like and it sucked. There's some cool stuff over here. I can show you, but like I need to, I need to tell. And so you start your journey, your story there, right? And as I'm telling the story, I'm talking about the result I want, like the journey of, of achievement. I want to achieve this really, really cool thing. And we started going into the desires. Now, with the desires, there's two kind of desires. There's the external desire that most of us are willing to share. Most of us are willing to share with people, right? Like, Oh, I want to lose weight or Oh, I want to make money or Oh, I want to, whatever that thing is, right? And most people only tell people around them, like, like we're all comfortable sharing our external desires. Okay. For all your business, like now let's use it really easy. The reality is like that's not really what people want to do stuff. Like I don't want to lose weight because I want to lose weight. I want to lose weight because of some other, like there's an internal desire. That's the real thing that I'm trying to get and trying to accomplish, right? And so usually to get to the internal, what I usually do, I'm asking someone if I'm trying to write a script for them or whatever, I start asking them why, like, well, why did you want to lose weight? Why did you want to end up keep asking why, why? And usually it's like five or six or seven wise till they break down and they become vulnerable. And they're like, this is why. And that's like the interesting thing. And so what happens is when you're telling your story and you come to your back, when you talk to like, look, I wanted to make money. I wanted to lose weight and you tell your external desires like, cool, that's what I want to do too. And as soon as you're willing to like say, but the real reason I want to do it is this and you share this deep internal desire, the same thing that almost everybody has, no one ever talks that level. And as soon as you do, it's like instant rapport with like, dang, I still want to, I've never told them that because I'm scared. They just told me and they were willing to tell me like, God, like in all sense, like instant, instant connection. So for you, it's, you understand in your story, like, here's the extra one I want, but then like digging deeper to figure out like why you actually wanted that. Like, and most of you guys know it sometimes subconsciously because you don't, we hide it, like we're taught to bury those kind of things. But if you ask enough, why is, why is, why is it'll come down to like, boom, I did this because I want, I want to impress my dad or all my wife to accept me or all my kids look up to me or whatever like the, those core desires are. Like that's the real reason. Okay. And if you, as you look at heroes to journeys, like the first journey is the, is going after the external desire and the journey of transformation is, is actually the internal desires. It's pretty cool when you, when you realize that, right? So that's the first one. So I'm sharing those things initially because I want, I want them to know that, that, that they, I feel what they feel. I've felt that before. And then that brings me out to this wall. So I have these desires. I have these things I want. Then I come to this wall and I'm stuck. And what I realized that morning a week and a half ago, guess what that wall is for everybody? It's the current opportunity you're in. It's the current opportunity they're in. So I'm in this wall. I'm in this opportunity. I've been trying to lose weight through this means, through this thing. And I can't do it anymore. I'm stuck. You were stuck. And you show like this is, I hit this wall. I was trying to, to get that result. I was stuck in this vehicle. I hit this wall. I can't get forward because the vehicle sucks. Like I cannot, it's impossible for me to move forward because the vehicle on me in. So for me to have success, I have to leave this vehicle. It's just scary because my whole life I was taught this is the way to do it. I have to get out of that vehicle and come over here to a new vehicle. And it's like, oh, scary. But I got to show them like I was willing to do that. Like my whole life I learned this is the way to do it. And as soon as I say, okay, I got it. I'm not getting my result. No matter how hard I try, I'm stuck in this wall. I'm stuck in this vehicle. I'm going to go on a journey because I have to figure this out. I have to get that. That's my goal. Then I walk over here. Boom. And so then I started doing something started happening. Okay. And that's where I started telling the story where I have this epiphany. And the epiphany I have is the new opportunity. Like I had this epiphany. Wow. I need this thing. And that brings down like that presents this new opportunity. So this is the new vehicle I get into. I'm like, God, this epiphany. I also realized that that was the thing. And I got into this new vehicle. Like sweet. I'm in this vehicle. It's different. I don't know if it's going to work yet. I just got here. Now what do I do? Okay. Well, I have a plan. What I'm going to do is I'm going to try this, this and this. And so I go on this plan. This is my plan to try to get that result. I'm going through this plan and somewhere through the plan there's this conflict that happens. I was trying to execute this conflict and I struggled when I couldn't get through because of this thing that happened. But then at the end, two things happened. Number one, it's I achieved it, right? I didn't achieve it. So I got my external desires, which is cool. But the coolest thing is not that the coolest thing. The car transformed the internal desires I had back there. That's what actually mattered. I'm showing them the resolution of the heroes two journeys. And that's the epiphany bridge script. Is that amazing? Oh, anyway, I got chills. I got chills just going through. Like I'm so excited. And so that's kind of the process. And so I also broke it down into questions that make it super easy for us to be able to have it, right? So the first thing, if you look through this, there's a couple elements. There's eight elements. Backstory, desires, the wall, the epiphany, the plan, the conflict, achieving and transformation. And I spent that night, if you guys watched my Snapchat, that's what I was doing until 4.30 that morning was this. I was just nuts and I'm like freaking out at Snapchat. I'm like, I can't figure out what's happening, but I'm freaking out. So I wrote these questions in a way that gets me to actually do the right thing, right? So the backstory. So what's your backstory that gives us a vested interest in your journey? It's not what's your backstory, but why should I care about you? So that's the first question. And then you answer that. Well, you tell the backstory that gives them a vested interest in your journey. Number two is what is the desire you wanted to accomplish? What were the external struggles you were dealing with? And what are the internal struggles? I'm going to share those. Then we have the wall. What's the wall or the problem you hit within your current opportunity that started you on this new journey? It was physically impossible for me to grow and get what I wanted because of this opportunity I'm stuck in. Then you had this epiphany that you experienced as you went on this journey. They introduced you to this new opportunity. You had a plan. What was the plan you created to achieve your desire? What was the conflict you experienced along the way? What was the result of what you achieved? And what was the transformation that you experienced? That's magic. Yeah. What do you guys think about that? It's going to take a while. Good. I'm going to go through and hopefully we won't mess up. I've got my notes. You will. I'm sure I will. Okay. Here we go. And we're going live in three, two, one. Hey everyone, this is Russell. Hope you guys are all doing awesome. So I'm here right now in the room of I think 48 or 49 amazing entrepreneurs. And you guys don't see me again. And we've been talking about storytelling and like how to tell your story and kind of the origin story of where you got started and what got you into what you're doing. And so I've been challenging all of them to go and do that. And I thought it would be kind of cool if I just told you guys my story. And so be as if for pieces or parts of this, but probably not the whole thing. And so what I'm going to do is just kind of share with you guys a story about click funnels and why this for me became my new opportunity. The thing that I'm so excited about and why I have so much passion. Some people say Russell, you've been talking about click funnels every day for the last two and a half years. I'm like, I know because I'm so excited every day. So some of the backstory if you don't know. So I got started in this business, I guess it was almost like 13 or 14 years ago now. And when I did, I was a college guys going to Boise State University, which is just down the road from it right now. And I was like a wrestler, which means I wrestled and I had to go to school. So I barely graduated. Like it was like barely. Like I think my acumen level is a 2.1, which means I got a whole bunch of C's and like one B over like five years. So it's kind of painful. But I graduated, which made my mom happy and was pretty cool. But during school, as I was wrestling, I started learning about this internet marketing stuff. And you have to remember this is 12 years ago pre, you know, it's before Facebook, it's before Instagram, all these kinds of things like we had Google kind of. And in fact, I don't remember how we were driving traffic. I don't remember anything like a website, anything like I was using front page. Some of the old timers remember front page. So I was creating these things in front page and it was, it was pretty bad. So I was doing that. But I believe that it was, it was possible. I saw people like some of my mentors were Armand Moran and Alex Mendoza and some of these guys who were making money online. I was like, God, these guys can do it. Like I'm pretty sure I can do it. So that was my goal I wanted to accomplish. Now the other side of that I don't really share a lot of times was it wasn't just that I wanted to make money. I had a desire I had, but like the bigger desires, I had just gotten married to my beautiful wife. We're still married now. She's 15 years this year, which is amazing. And I married her. She was working full time to support me, her jobless wrestling husband. And I don't know about you, but like I was a vision like when I got married, I'd be like the man and I would be like the supporter. And it was, it was honestly pretty tough for me that she was supporting me and I didn't have anything to contribute other than like, I'm going to go to school and wrestle and hopefully, and it was hard for me. And I remember like, I kind of figured out a way to contribute. I don't want her doing everything. And it was, it was, uh, and so that was like the real driving factor. Like if I can, in fact, my goal is if I can make a thousand dollars a month, that would like match what she was making. We weren't making very much money at the time. I was like, then like, I feel like I'm contributing as well. And so that was my goal. Like I need to figure out how to do this. And I tried all sorts of stuff I was doing. Um, I was trying eBay. I was trying, uh, Craig's, I was doing stuff. We had like little like, I had like a $20 a month, which was like big deal for me. I was excited, but like none, none of the things really hit big. And about the time I started learning about information marketing, in fact, I was at, uh, I was at a post office mailing something I'd bought from eBay in this huge awkward box. I thought it was going to sell huge and, uh, I didn't sell it like a buck 50 or something. I lost tons of money. And then I did a postage and it was a nightmare. I'm sitting in the line at the post office next to this dude who had a big box full of CDs, like thousands of them. I'm like, dude, what are you selling? He's like, well, I have one CD I sell and I burn it. And I'm like, so these are orders for like the month. He's like, no, these are my orders for this week. I'm like, I'm doing the math. How much you selling for? It's like 67 bucks. Oh my gosh. And I had this like, this, like, I need to sell information like that is the future. So I started trying to figure out information. I started doing stuff. And my very first product I ever created was a DVD on how to make potato guns. I sure some of you guys have, have heard this story before. So I started making potato guns and it was working really good. But then what I was doing is I was going to Google, I was buying ads on Google and we were making some money back and forth. But eventually I probably had four or five months of success and then Google changed their algorithm and it got really, really hard. The harder thing for me was like, during this time, like, it was me building things in front pages and I had to figure out how to like FTP pages and images and then how to go to PayPal and get the order button. It was like, it was super techie and I'm not a techie person at all. But after Google changed our algorithm, we tried some stuff and I remember one of my friends called me, Mike Philson, some of you guys may know Mike and he said, he said, Russell, I figured out like how to make this whole internet marketing game work again. I'm like, what do you mean? Is your potato gun thing still making money? No, like I can't, I can't break even, I can't make a profit. And he's like, well, I went back to all my sites and I added these things and he called them OTOs. I was like, what's an OTO? He's like, it's an upsell and one time offer. I'm like, oh, and so he's like, I had these upsells and people started buying the upsells. And suddenly like I'm making two or three times as much money from everything I sold. And I was like, dude, I can actually do that. And so I remember jumping online. I was Googling like potato guns and trying to figure out like what would my upsell be? I don't know. Like all I had was a potato gun DVD and I met this dude up in Northern Idaho who actually made potato guns and dropped shipped him. And I was like, dude, if I sell those, will you drop ship him for me? He was like, yeah, like I love to not have to sell these things. And so I partnered with him and I started selling DVDs and people started buying this upsell. And it was the first thing I had made a funnel and I was like, this is a funnel like, and that was like this new thing. And I was so excited. And so I had this epiphany like this is the future. Like if I can make more funnels like this funnels will make me like, I don't know, 20, 30 bucks a day. But what if I had two funnels or three or five or 10? And so I started going a little bit crazy and I created funnels in the couponing market, which by the way is a horrible market. People in that market do not like to spend money. So I don't do that one. But I did coupon and we did dating. We did weight loss. We did network marketing. I've done diabetic supplements. We've done and that's like the ones on top of my head. Like there's like, I show you guys, there's like a trail of thousands of offers. Some of the work and a lot more that didn't work. But we were doing these things over and over and my plan was like, if I had 10 of them, they'd be making a hundred bucks a day or 20 or 50 or whatever it is. So I was doing this. And at first it was working, but like the problem I kept running into was like front pages for something like me was hard and I had like to do these pages and they might look ugly. Other people's like, look awesome. So then I had a designer. He would design these things. So I'd make it in front pages ugly. I sent him, he designed it. Look cool. And I put it up, but then it was up and then I couldn't do anything. I couldn't touch it again. Like I had one shot and then I had like bug him. So then I hired like a web guy to go and like do edits for me. And then like I was just using PayPal and I couldn't do anything else. And so then someone like invented like a one click upsell. So I was like, dude, I need that. So I had to hire a programmer to connect that to the next thing. And like it kept getting more and more complicated to the point where like, soon I wasn't able to do anything. I just look at him and be like, change that. And then they try to change it and they didn't. I'm like, no, they move it over here and then they put it in the wrong spot. And like it would take days, sometimes weeks going back and forth with the guys in Romania and India and everywhere trying to get them just to move an image and make it a little bit bigger or like things that like were so common sense, but I couldn't actually do it. And it drove me nuts. And about that time, one of my friends and partners, in fact, he may not be in the room right now, is Todd in here still? Oh, he's already eating lunch. He's already gone. But Todd was like one of my partners that was doing these things with me and he saw my frustration. He's like, what if we built something where like, and the joke was like that Russell could actually like edit the website and actually could move things. We quit bugging us all the time. And I was like, dude, that'd be awesome. He's like, okay, that's what we're going to create. And that day we sat in front of a white board show and mapped out like, what would it look like? How would it work? And so we mapped out this vision of what became ClickFunnels and he went home and actually built it. And then it was funny him. And then another part we brought in was Dylan Jones. They were working. He did the editor. And they would build part of it. And they're like, we need to like make this so simple. Russell can do it. So they do it and they say, Russell, test this. And I log in and I'm like, how do I do it? He's like, we're not going to tell you. You got to figure it out on your own. I'm like, okay. So I'd like do some stuff. And if I could figure it out, then like, good, we got it. If I got stuck, I'm like, I don't know what to do. Then like, okay, we didn't do it intuitively enough. So I went back and forth and back and forth for probably six or seven months. And finally it was a point where like, I got it. I was like, if I can do this, like then anyone can. And first it was just a tool for me. Like that was really the initial goal of ClickFunnels. Like I think it was it taught and don't like this way Russell will quit bugging us and I don't have to design sites anymore or redo his headers. Like all this stuff that was happening. And then after I started using it and I was having success, I was like, we have to make this a tool for everyone. Like it'll free all entrepreneurs and like it freed me. And so because of that, I was able to start creating funnels quickly. I went from having five or six guys and take us on average almost two to three months every single time to build a funnel where I was actually able to do it by myself in like an hour, an hour and a half sometimes. But then it's less, which was cool. And so the cool thing is like I was able to achieve that and have success. We started rolling out funnels like crazy. Some of you guys probably remember seeing a lot of them. I still do a lot of them because it's so much fun to do. But the cooler thing for me was the other side. Like I was able to make money to support my wife and support my kids because I didn't no longer had to spend three months doing something. My time to be with my kids is completely freed up. Like I have a chance to like go home every day and play with my kids. I'm not like stressing out, trying to like talk to programmers in India at three in the morning and like stressing all the stuff. Like it just works. And that was really the biggest thing for me. And it's been an amazing thing. In fact now my kids are using click funnels. They're building stuff in click funnels. And it's like not just, for me it's not just a product. It's a mission. I'm seeing people's lives change. I'm seeing other people who had messages but they couldn't get them out because they couldn't move things around on this stupid page. We're now like the power is back in their hand. And that's what fires me up is being able to help entrepreneurs and that kind of thing. So that's kind of my click funnel story. So if you guys haven't heard it before I want to share that with you guys and to share why I'm so passionate about this. And you know for me it's not just, I talk about this all the time but like it's not a matter if you're going to become a click funnels member. It's when. Because it's the only way to do what we're talking about. Like the only alternative is to do what I used to do ten years ago and hire guys in Romania and India and the Philippines. I think have a team of 30 people to be able to move an image. Or you can just use click funnels and it gives you and me the entrepreneurs the power back which is awesome. So I hope that helps you guys. And I hope you guys see why I'm so passionate about click funnels. And with that said I'm going to check out. And if you like this comment down below. If you have any friends who are like what is that click fun only thing. Share this to them. Tag them down below so they can see why me, why I'm so passionate and probably why you're so passionate too. So thanks you guys. Talk to you guys all soon. Bye.
